solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1431F - Neural Network Problem
Binary search is pretty obvious even from the problem statement. Let's fix the maximum sum of the block $$$sum$$$. Now we have to check if we can remove at most $$$k$$$ elements to obtain some array where each block has the sum at most $$$sum$$$.
To check it, let's implement the following greedy solution: go from left to right and maintain the priority queue of $$$x$$$ minimums. Suppose we are at the position $$$i$$$ now. Add the current element $$$a_i$$$ to the priority queue and remove the biggest element from it while its size is greater than $$$x$$$ or the sum is greater than $$$sum$$$. If the size of the queue is $$$x$$$ it also means that its sum is not greater than $$$sum$$$. Let's end the current block here (just clear the queue) and start a new one from the next position. In general, we are trying to complete the block as soon as possible. It is always optimal because the earlier we end the block — the more blocks we can create (the fewer elements we have to remove). After processing all elements, let's check if the number of elements removed is not greater than $$$k$$$.
Time complexity: $$$O(n \log^2{n})$$$.

solutions.title: Solution (vovuh)
solutions.content:

.

(

,
x
)
=

toInt
()

=

split
(

map

.
toInt
()

=
0L

10L
*

*

1000

=

(

:

:

{

=

=

var

=
PriorityQueue
<
Int

<

>
{
-
it
})
for
(

in
0
until

)
{
cursum
+=
a
[
i
]
cur
.
add
(
a
[
i
])
while
(
cur
.
size
>
x
||
cursum
>
sum
)
{
cursum
-=
cur
.
first
()
cur
.
remove
()
need
+=
1
}
if
(

.
size
==
x
)
{
cursum
=
0L
cur
.
clear
()
}
}
return
need
<=
k
}
while
(
l
<=
r
)
{
val mid
=
(
l
+

)
/
2
if
(
can
(
mid
))
{
res
=
mid
            r
=
mid
-
1
}
else
{
l
=
mid
+
1
}
}
println
(
res
)
